claim: Metastases have genomic aberrations different than those of the primary tumor.

result.claim: Metastases have genomic aberrations different than those of the primary tumor.
result.reasoning: Multiple studies provide strong evidence that metastases exhibit genomic aberrations that are different from those of the primary tumor. Paper ID 21176485 found 157 statistically significant differences between primary lesions and matched metastases in breast cancer, with primary tumors displaying a 20% higher number of aberrations. Paper ID 30103626 suggests that disseminated tumor cells acquire genomic aberrations typical of metastatic cells after leaving the primary tumor, indicating an evolutionary divergence. Paper ID 36352393 explicitly states that recent studies have revealed "substantial discordances between primary tumors and metastases... on the genomic and transcriptomic level." Furthermore, Paper ID 162172233 indicates that metastatic breast cancers show an increase in mutational burden and clonal diversity compared to early breast cancers, leading to a more complex genomic landscape. Finally, Paper ID 52158902 demonstrated "considerable genetic heterogeneity between primary lung cancer and corresponding brain metastases specimens," although it also noted consistent high frequencies of mutation for EGFR and TP53, suggesting that while some mutations may be shared, overall genomic profiles differ significantly.

evidence.text: Reduced DUSP4 expression in primary tumors after NAC was associated with shorter recurrence-free survival.
evidence.source: Profiling of residual breast cancers after neoadjuvant chemotherapy identifies DUSP4 deficiency as a mechanism of drug resistance

evidence.text: EGFR consistently exhibited high frequencies of mutation between primary tumors and corresponding brain metastases.
evidence.source: Characterization of genetic alterations in brain metastases from non‐small cell lung cancer

evidence.text: Disseminated tumor cells from M0-stage patients displayed significantly fewer chromosomal aberrations than primary tumors (P < 0.008).
